attributes.stem:
A 36-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of worsening psychotic symptoms. He reports hearing voices telling him to harm himself and believes that his neighbours are spying on him through his television. He was diagnosed with schizophrenia six months ago and has had two eight-week trials of antipsychotic medications. He first tried olanzapine orally. This was changed to a paliperidone monthly intramuscular depot administered by his case manager. Neither medication provided significant symptom relief. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears agitated with a pulse rate of 100/min, blood pressure 130/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He is seen by the psychiatry consultation liaison team who recommends starting him on the next line of antipsychotic treatment.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate set of initial investigations prior to starting this medication?
options: Liver function tests, renal profile, fasting lipids, and HbA1c
Thyroid function tests, prolactin level, CT head, and EEG
Creatinine kinase, C-reactive protein, BNP, and lipase
Complete blood examination, electrocardiogram, echocardiogram, and troponin
Valproate level, prolactin level,  vitamin B12, and folate
attributes.explanation:
This patient has most likely been recommended to start clozapine. Clozapine is an atypical antipsychotic medication used for treatment-resistant schizophrenia, which is defined as failure of two trials of antipsychotic medications. Clozapine is effective in the treatment of schizophrenia but is not first-line due to its wide range of side effects. As a result, careful monitoring of patients on clozapine is essential.

The most appropriate set of initial investigations prior to starting clozapine includes a complete blood examination, electrocardiogram, echocardiogram, and troponin. These tests are crucial due to the potential serious side effects of clozapine, particularly agranulocytosis and cardiotoxicity. A complete blood examination is necessary to check for baseline haematological parameters, particularly the white blood cell count and absolute neutrophil count, due to the risk of agranulocytosis (occurring in about 1% of patients). An electrocardiogram (ECG) is important to assess baseline cardiac conduction, as clozapine can cause QT interval prolongation. An echocardiogram and troponin are included to evaluate cardiac function and rule out any pre-existing cardiac issues, given the risk of clozapine-induced myocarditis (occurring in less than 3% of patients).

Liver function tests, renal profile, fasting lipids, and HbA1c are important tests but are typically part of ongoing monitoring rather than initial investigations. While clozapine can affect liver function, cause metabolic side effects, and rarely impact renal function, these are usually monitored during treatment rather than before initiation.

Thyroid function tests, prolactin level, CT head, and EEG are not routinely required before starting clozapine. While clozapine can cause hypothyroidism, this is typically monitored during treatment. Prolactin levels are not directly related to clozapine's primary side effect profile. CT head and EEG are not routinely performed unless there are neurological symptoms or concerns.

Creatinine kinase, C-reactive protein, BNP, and lipase are tests that are more relevant during ongoing clozapine treatment rather than before initiation. Creatinine Kinase and C-reactive protein can be used to monitor for myocarditis. BNP is not routinely monitored in clozapine treatment. Lipase is not typically relevant unless there are concerns about pancreatitis.

Valproate level, prolactin level, vitamin B12, and folate are not typically required before starting clozapine unless there are specific clinical indications. Valproate levels are not relevant when starting clozapine. Vitamin B12 and folate are not directly related to clozapine's side effect profile.

It's important to note that once clozapine is initiated, ongoing monitoring includes regular complete blood examinations, metabolic parameters (blood glucose, lipid panel), cardiac monitoring, and serum clozapine levels. These investigations are typically organized through a multidisciplinary team in a clozapine clinic to ensure comprehensive care and early detection of potential side effects.
learningPoints: The most important initial investigations prior to starting clozapine therapy in patients with treatment-resistant schizophrenia include a complete blood examination, electrocardiogram, echocardiogram, and troponin due to the risks of agranulocytosis and cardiotoxicity.
Regular monitoring of haematological, cardiac, and metabolic parameters is essential throughout clozapine treatment.
references: Haidary HA, Padhy RK. Clozapine. StatPearls. Treasure Island (FL)2020.

Galletly C, Castle D, Dark F, Humberstone V, Jablensky A, Killackey E, Kulkarni J, McGorry P, Nielssen O, Tran N. Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia and related disorders. Australian & New Zealand Journal of Psychiatry. 2016 May;50(5):410-72.
